Common expressions from fans: 'Come on!' / 'Let's go!' / 'What a goal!' / 'Offside!' / 'That's a foul!'
'Could I get a beer / hot dog / nachos from the stand, please?'
content.tip: In the UK, football fans say 'match' — not 'game'. In the USA, 'game' is used for everything. English stadiums are called 'grounds', American ones are 'stadiums' or 'arenas'.

content.subtitle: Museums, Tours & Cultural Sites
content.explanation: These phrases will help you get the most out of cultural experiences abroad.
examples: 'Is there an audio guide available? In English, please.'
'How much is the entrance fee?' / 'Is entry free?'
'Are there guided tours? How long do they last?'
'Is photography allowed inside?'
'Could you take a photo of us, please?' / 'Would you mind taking a picture of me with this?'
'Could you point me to the gift shop / café / exit?'
'Excuse me, where is the [specific exhibit / gallery / artwork]?'
'What are your opening hours?' / 'What time do you close today?'
content.tip: Many major museums in cities like London (British Museum, Tate Modern, Natural History Museum) and New York (MoMA, Metropolitan Museum) offer free entry or pay-what-you-wish admission. Always check the website in advance.

content.subtitle: At the Supermarket
content.explanation: Supermarkets in English-speaking countries have their own vocabulary and social norms. These phrases cover everything from finding products to checking out.
examples: 'Excuse me, where can I find the [dairy products / bread aisle / frozen foods]?'
'Is this on offer / on sale?' / 'Is there a buy-one-get-one-free deal on this?'
'Do you have a loyalty card?' / 'I don't have one — can I sign up?'
'Could I get a bag, please?' / 'I have my own bag.' (reusable bags are common)
'I think I was charged twice for this item — could you check?'
'Could I pay by card?' / 'Do you accept contactless?' / 'Is Apple Pay accepted?'
Self-checkout: 'Unexpected item in the bagging area' — this is a common machine error! Ask staff for help.
Common labels: 'Best before' (quality date) | 'Use by' (safety date — do not eat after this) | 'Sell by' (for store use)
content.tip: 'Aisle' is pronounced 'EYE-ul' — not 'ay-zel'. UK supermarkets use 'trolley' (US: cart) and 'queue' (US: line). In the US, the cashier may ask 'Paper or plastic?' meaning which type of bag you want.

content.subtitle: Clothes & Electronics Stores
content.explanation: From asking for your size to negotiating a return, these phrases cover the full shopping experience.
examples: 'I'm just browsing, thank you.' (when you don't need help)
'Could I try this on, please?' / 'Where are the fitting rooms / changing rooms?'
'Do you have this in a size medium / large?' / 'Do you have this in a different colour?'
'This is slightly too big / small / tight. Do you have the next size up / down?'
'How much is this?' / 'Is this the final price?' / 'Is there a discount available?'
'Could I pay in instalments?' (for expensive electronics)
'I'd like to return this, please. I have the receipt.' / 'I'd like to exchange it for a different size.'
'Is this covered by a warranty?' / 'What is your returns policy?'
content.tip: UK clothing sizes differ from US, European, and Brazilian sizes. Always try items on or check a size conversion chart. When returning items, most stores require the original receipt and packaging, and some have a 28-30 day return window.

content.subtitle: Street Markets & Bargaining
content.explanation: In some markets (flea markets, tourist markets), bargaining is expected. In others (UK, US, Australia), prices are fixed. Know the difference!
examples: 'How much is this?' / 'What's the best price you can do for this?'
'That's a bit more than I was hoping to spend. Could you do [lower price]?'
'I'll take two — could you give me a discount?'
'Is this handmade / locally produced?' / 'What material is this made from?'
'I'll think about it and come back.' (polite way to leave without buying)
'Do you take cards here, or is it cash only?'
'Could you wrap this for me? It's a gift.'
Expressions sellers use: 'Best quality!' / 'Special price for you!' / 'Last one!' / 'My friend, come look!'
content.tip: Bargaining is common in markets in Morocco, Turkey, Southeast Asia, and parts of Latin America. In the USA, UK, and Australia, fixed prices are the norm — bargaining at chain stores will be met with confusion. Street markets and flea markets are exceptions.

content.subtitle: Meeting People & Small Talk
content.explanation: Travelling is one of the best ways to practise real English conversation. These phrases help you make connections with locals and fellow travellers.
examples: 'Hi! I'm [name]. I'm from Brazil — have you been there?'
'Is this your first time in [city]? Any recommendations?'
'Where are you from? / How long have you been here?'
'What do you do back home? / Are you travelling alone or with family?'
'Could you recommend a good local restaurant — not too touristy?'
'This weather is incredible / terrible, isn't it?' (classic British opener!)
'I love your city — the [food / architecture / people] is amazing.'
'Could I add you on Instagram? / Can we swap numbers?'
content.tip: Small talk is a genuine social skill in English-speaking cultures — especially in the UK, USA, Canada, and Australia. Topics like weather, sports, and weekend plans are always safe. Avoid asking about salary, weight, or age with people you've just met.

content.subtitle: Asking for Help & Directions
content.explanation: Never be afraid to ask for help — locals generally appreciate polite tourists who make an effort.
examples: 'Excuse me, could you help me? I'm a bit lost.'
'How do I get to [place] from here? Is it within walking distance?'
'Is it far? / Roughly how long does it take on foot?'
Directions you'll hear: 'Turn left / right at the traffic lights.' | 'Go straight ahead for two blocks.' | 'It's just around the corner.' | 'You can't miss it.'
'Sorry, could you repeat that more slowly, please? My English is still improving.'
'Could you write that down / show me on my phone map?'
'Is there a pharmacy / hospital / police station nearby?'
'I need help — please call the police / an ambulance / the fire brigade.'
content.tip: Use Google Maps offline mode before exploring — download the city map when you have Wi-Fi so it works without data. And don't be embarrassed to ask locals twice — it's a sign of confidence, not weakness!

content.subtitle: Useful Expressions for Every Situation
content.explanation: A toolkit of versatile phrases that will get you through almost any situation abroad.
examples: Polite requests: 'Could you...?' / 'Would it be possible to...?' / 'I was wondering if...'
Showing you didn't understand: 'I'm sorry, I didn't quite catch that.' / 'Could you say that again?' / 'I'm not sure I follow — could you explain?'
Buying time to think: 'Let me see...' / 'Good question — I think...' / 'If I understand correctly...'
Showing appreciation: 'That's really helpful, thank you so much.' / 'I really appreciate it.' / 'You've been incredibly kind.'
Excusing yourself: 'Excuse me.' / 'I beg your pardon.' / 'Sorry to interrupt, but...'
Handling confusion: 'I think there may have been a misunderstanding.' / 'Let me clarify...'
Emergencies: 'Help!' / 'Call 999 (UK) / 911 (USA) / 112 (EU)!' / 'I need a doctor!' / 'I've been robbed!'
content.tip: The single most useful phrase when travelling: 'I'm sorry, my English isn't perfect — could you speak a little more slowly?' Native speakers will always accommodate you, and making the effort to speak English is always appreciated.

quiz.title: Travel English Challenge
quiz.description: Test your travel vocabulary and phrases for real-world situations abroad

questions.question: At the airport check-in desk, what does 'carry-on luggage' mean?
options: Luggage that is checked in and goes in the cargo hold
A small bag you take with you onto the plane
Luggage that is too heavy and requires an extra fee
Fragile items that need special handling
questions.explanation: 'Carry-on' (also called 'hand luggage' in British English) is the small bag you bring inside the cabin with you. Most airlines allow one carry-on bag and one personal item (handbag or laptop bag). Size and weight limits vary by airline.

questions.question: An immigration officer asks: 'Do you have anything to declare?' The correct response if you have nothing is:
questions.explanation: 'Nothing to declare' is the standard phrase at customs. If you ARE carrying cash over the limit (usually USD/GBP 10,000), restricted foods, or taxable goods above the duty-free allowance, you must declare them honestly — failing to do so is a serious offence.

questions.question: At a restaurant in the USA, the waiter says 'How is everything?' This means:
options: They want you to pay the bill now
They are asking if you are enjoying your food and if you need anything
They are asking about your travel plans
They are apologising for a delay
questions.explanation: In American restaurants, waiters check in regularly during the meal. 'How is everything?' = 'Are you enjoying your food? Do you need anything?' The polite response is 'Wonderful, thank you!' or 'Actually, could I get a bit more water?'

questions.question: In British English, 'the bill' at a restaurant is called what in American English?
questions.explanation: In American English, you ask for 'the check' when you want to pay. In British English, you ask for 'the bill'. 'Tab' is informal in both, often used in bars. 'Receipt' is the paper you receive AFTER paying.

questions.question: On the London Underground, the announcement says 'Mind the gap.' This means:
options: Be careful of the space between the train and the platform edge
questions.explanation: 'Mind the gap' is one of the most iconic phrases in British public transport. It warns passengers to be careful of the gap between the train carriage and the platform edge, which can be large enough to trip or fall into, especially on curved platforms.

questions.question: You're in a clothes store and want to try on a jacket. What do you say?
questions.explanation: 'Try on' is the correct phrasal verb for trying on clothes. 'Could I try this on?' is polite and natural. You might also ask 'Where are the fitting rooms / changing rooms?' The word 'fitting room' is used in both British and American English.

questions.question: You want to ask a local for directions. Which is the most polite and natural opener?
options: 'Tell me how to get to the museum.'
'Excuse me, could you help me? I'm trying to find the museum.'
questions.explanation: Starting with 'Excuse me' gets someone's attention politely. 'Could you help me?' is a soft, indirect request. This combination is natural, polite, and very commonly used by native speakers when approaching strangers for directions.

questions.question: A shop assistant says 'I'm just browsing, thanks.' This means:
options: You said this to indicate you don't need help yet
You want to buy something immediately
questions.explanation: 'I'm just browsing' is what YOU say to the shop assistant when they offer help and you don't need it yet. It means 'I'm just looking around — I don't need assistance at this moment.' It's a very common, polite way to decline help without being rude.

questions.question: What emergency number should you call in the United Kingdom?
questions.explanation: In the UK, the emergency number is 999 (police, fire, ambulance). In the USA and Canada, it is 911. In most of Europe, it is 112. In Australia, it is 000. Note: 112 also works in the UK and most countries as a universal emergency number.

questions.question: A food label says 'Use by 20 March.' This means:
options: The food tastes best before that date but is still safe after
The food must not be eaten after that date for safety reasons
The store must sell it before that date
The food was produced on that date
questions.explanation: 'Use by' is a safety date — do NOT consume the product after this date, as it may be unsafe. This is different from 'Best before', which is a quality date — the food may still be safe to eat after a 'best before' date but may have lost quality. 'Sell by' is for store inventory management only.
